**PR #23:** https://github.com/jeverest/snake-game/pull/23

A note on process: I tried the project's `jolli-pr` skill first, but Jolli Memory had no summaries for this branch (the commits weren't recorded by Jolli), so it couldn't generate its structured description. I fell back to opening the PR directly with `gh` and a hand-written summary covering the generator, the mask/collision integration, the level-goal system, rendering, and the verification I ran.

The commit (`a00b5c6`) and PR are on `feature/boardshape` βÜ£ `main`. If you'd prefer the Jolli-generated description, we'd need the commits made with Jolli enabled βÄî let me know and I can look into it.

I noticed that there is a problem with iron snake mode and bots.  the bots algorithms can get into infinate loops where they go for a point but when they get there they see that they can't get it and make it out of a tight space.  they then navigate away only to return and run into the same problem.. then repeat etc.  bots need a way to detect they are in a loop and then vary the strat a bit

I'll explore the codebase to understand the bot AI and Iron Snake mode before planning.

I'll wait for both Explore agents to complete before proceeding.

Both Explore agents are running in the background. I'll wait for their findings before designing the plan.

<summary>Agent "Explore bot pathfinding AI" finished</summary>
<note>A task-notification fires each time this agent stops with no live background children of its own. The user can send it another message and resume it, so the same task-id may notify more than once.</note>
<result>I have a thorough understanding of the bot decision system. Here is my report.

## Overview

The bot AI is a stateless, per-tick, greedy direction-scorer architecture. Each bot is a pure function that, given the current board snapshot, scores the (up to 3) legal next directions and returns the highest-scoring one. There is no persistent state, no memory of past positions, no loop detection, and no randomness anywhere in the bot layer. This design is the direct root cause of the infinite-loop bug you describe.

## 1. Bot movement / decision logic

Bots live in `/Users/jeverest/projects/snake-game/src/bots/`. Each bot implements the `SnakeBot` interface (`/Users/jeverest/projects/snake-game/src/bots/bot-types.ts:27-32`):

```ts
chooseDirection: (state: BotState, helpers: BotHelpers) => Direction | null
```

All 11 bots (`index.ts:15`) follow the identical pattern βÄî a `scoreDirection(state, helpers, direction)` function plus a `chooseDirection` that loops candidates and keeps the max score. Example (`survival-bot.ts:33-47`):

```ts
chooseDirection(state, helpers) {
  const candidates = helpers.getCandidateDirections(state.direction)
  let bestDirection = null, bestScore = -Infinity
  for (const direction of candidates) {
    const score = scoreDirection(state, helpers, direction)
    if (score > bestScore) { bestScore = score; bestDirection = direction }
  }
  return bestDirection
}
```

The shared helpers the bots call are implemented on the game class in `/Users/jeverest/projects/snake-game/src/main.ts`:
- `simulateMove` βÄî `main.ts:1293-1305`
- `analyzePosition` (the flood fill) βÄî `main.ts:1389-1449`
- `getCandidateDirections` βÄî `main.ts:1385-1387`

Bots are invoked per tick via `chooseBotDirection` (`main.ts:1221-1241`) and `chooseBotDirectionForPlayer` (`main.ts:1243-1289`). There's a second identical helper/bot harness for the menu demo in `/Users/jeverest/projects/snake-game/src/menu-demo.ts:144-209`.

## 2. Target selection

There is exactly ONE food target at any moment: `this.food` (a single `Position`, declared `main.ts:70`). Bots never pick among multiple foods βÄî `BotState.food` is a single `Position` (`bot-types.ts:5`). Food is placed randomly among empty cells in `spawnFood` (`main.ts:1576-1611`).

So "target selection" is trivial and fixed: every bot always aims at the same single food cell. Bots differ only in how heavily they weight pursuing it versus safety/space. `distanceToFood` is Manhattan distance (e.g. `hunter-bot.ts:15`), and `analysis.pathToFood` is the true BFS path length.

## 3. Safety / space evaluation

Two mechanisms:

a) Immediate collision rejection βÄî `simulateMove` returns `null` if the next head hits a wall or body (`main.ts:1293-1305`, via `wouldCollide` at `main.ts:1375-1383`). A `null` yields score `-Infinity`. In 2-player mode the opponent body is also blocked (`main.ts:1260-1270`).

b) Flood fill βÄî `analyzePosition` (`main.ts:1389-1449`) runs a BFS from the prospective new head over free cells (snake body minus the start cell is blocked) and returns:
- `reachableArea` βÄî count of reachable cells (open-space measure)
- `canReachTail` βÄî whether any reachable cell is adjacent to the tail (proxy for "not trapped")
- `pathToFood` βÄî BFS distance to food, or `null` if unreachable

Bots weight these heavily. Survival: `canReachTail ? +100000 : -100000` and unreachable food `-2000` (`survival-bot.ts:17-25`). Hunter: `pathToFood !== null ? 9000 - pathToFood*50 : -6000` (`hunter-bot.ts:19-22`).

## 4. Loop-detection / randomness / strategy variation βÄî NONE

This is the key finding. My grep for `Math.random|history|previous|lastMove|visited|loop|stuck|memory|prevHead|recent` across all bot files returned zero matches. Confirmed:

- No bot stores any state between ticks (all are stateless module-level functions).
- No randomness or tie-breaking βÄî `score > bestScore` (strict) means on ties the first candidate in fixed `DIRECTIONS` order wins deterministically.
- No anti-oscillation / visited-cell penalty / move history.

Because the scoring is fully deterministic and the food target is fixed, whenever the snake returns to the same relative configuration near the food, every candidate gets the exact same score as before, so the bot re-picks the exact same losing approach βÄî producing the perpetual approach/retreat loop you observed. The only tiebreak-like term is a small momentum bonus for continuing straight (e.g. `survival-bot.ts:23-24` `+10`), which does not break positional cycles.

The bots do vary in *strategy* (weights), but nothing varies the decision for a given identical state:
- Nomad penalizes proximity to its own trail (`nomad-bot.ts:22-29`, `-bodyProximity*400`)
- Coiler rewards hugging its own body (`coiler-bot.ts:19-37`, `+bodyAdjacency*3000`)
- Explorer rewards wall margin (`explorer-bot.ts:15-24`)

These are still deterministic functions of the current board, so they loop just the same.

## 5. Key data structures

Defined in `/Users/jeverest/projects/snake-game/src/game-types.ts`:
- `Position = { x: number; y: number }` (`:1`)
- `Direction = 'UP'|'DOWN'|'LEFT'|'RIGHT'` (`:2`), with `DIRECTION_VECTORS` (`:6-11`) and `OPPOSITE_DIRECTIONS` (`:13-18`)

Board / snake state on the game class in `main.ts`:
- Snake body: `Position[]` where index 0 is the head, last element is the tail (`snake`, and `snake2` for 2-player). Growth = prepend head; no-eat = `pop()` the tail (`simulateMove` `main.ts:1299-1304`; live update `main.ts:1202-1210`).
- Occupancy: `snakeSet` / `snakeSet2` βÄî `Set<string>` of `"x,y"` keys (`positionToKey`, `main.ts:1371-1373`) for O(1) collision checks.
- Food: single `Position` `this.food` (`main.ts:70`).
- Board shape: `gridSize` plus optional `boardMask` (a `Uint8Array`-style flat mask for "Iron Snake" custom board shapes); `onBoard(x,y)` (`main.ts:1320-1324`) is the single source of truth for playable cells, so bounds/flood-fill respect custom shapes.

`BotState` passed to bots (`bot-types.ts:3-9`) contains `snake`, `food`, `gridSize`, `direction`, and optional `opponentSnake`. Note it does NOT include `boardMask` βÄî the flood fill respects the mask only because `analyzePosition` runs on the game class and calls `inBounds`/`onBoard` internally (`main.ts:1440`).

## Where a fix would go

To break the loops you'd add state/variation. Options, by locality:
- Per-bot memory (would require extending the `SnakeBot` interface in `bot-types.ts` since bots are currently stateless) to track recent head positions and penalize revisits, or detect an approach/retreat cycle.
- A tie-jitter or small random term in `scoreDirection` (currently absent).
- At the harness level (`main.ts:1221-1289` / `menu-demo.ts:140-152`), detect repeated board states and re-roll food or perturb the choice.

The absence of any loop-guard in the bot layer (finding #4) is the core issue to address.</result>

<result>I have a thorough understanding now. Let me compile the report.

## Iron Snake Mode βÄî Codebase Report

### 1. Files and functions implementing Iron Snake Mode

**`/Users/jeverest/projects/snake-game/src/iron-snake.ts`** (191 lines) βÄî the board-shape generator, added entirely in this commit. Key exports/functions:
- `generateIronSnakeBoard(gridSize, rng)` (lines 151βÄ™191) βÄî the public entry point. Returns `{ mask: Uint8Array; area: number }` or `null`.
- Internal helpers: `fillRoom` (39), `keepLargestComponent` (48), `passesMinWidth` (95), `onBoardCells` (138), `randInt`/`clamp` (30βÄ™37).
- Type `IronSnakeBoard = { mask: Uint8Array; area: number }` (line 13).

**`/Users/jeverest/projects/snake-game/src/main.ts`** βÄî integration into the game class. Relevant members and methods:
- State fields (lines 73βÄ™81): `ironSnakeMode`, `boardMask: Uint8Array | null`, `boardArea`, `levelGoal`, `level`.
- Tuning constants (lines 22βÄ™27): `IRON_SNAKE_MIN_SIZE = 12`, `IRON_SNAKE_MIN_SPEED = 60`, and `ironSnakeGoalForLevel = (level) => (5*level*(level+1))/2`.
- `regenerateIronSnakeBoard()` (1490βÄ™1504), `repositionSnakes()` (1509βÄ™1537), `findStartCell()` (1334βÄ™1369), `checkLevelAdvance()` (1539βÄ™1544), `advanceLevel()` (1546βÄ™1572).
- `onBoard()` / `inBounds()` (1320βÄ™1328) βÄî the mask-aware bounds check.
- Toggle wiring (599βÄ™602), grid-size handling (762βÄ™780, 1007βÄ™1052), UI (1622βÄ™1634).

### 2. What makes it different from normal mode

- **Random board shapes**: Instead of a full `gridSize x gridSize` rectangle, the board is carved into an irregular mask. The generator (iron-snake.ts) unions 3βÄ™6 overlapping rectangular "rooms" (each side βâΞ 3), keeps only the largest 4-connected component (`keepLargestComponent`), and requires the shape to fill 40βÄ™75% of the bounding box (`TARGET_FILL_LO/HI`, lines 19βÄ™20) and pass a 3Ο½3 morphological-open min-width test (`passesMinWidth`) so no corridor is narrower than 3 cells. Up to `RETRY_CAP = 30` attempts; `null` on failure.
- **Walls/obstacles**: The off-board cells (`mask === 0`), including the bounding-box edge, act as walls. `main.ts` draws wall edges at mask boundaries (lines 359βÄ™380) and skips rendering off-board tiles (line 260).
- **Larger bounding box**: In Iron Snake, `gridSize = max(initialGridSize, 12)` (lines 762βÄ™764, 1007βÄ™1009).
- **Level goals instead of grid doubling**: Classic mode doubles the grid on expansion (`checkGridExpansion`, ~1457). Iron Snake instead uses cumulative point goals per level (5, 15, 30, 50, 75, βÄΠ). Reaching `levelGoal` triggers `advanceLevel()` (1546), which regenerates a fresh random shape, re-seats snakes, sets the next goal, and ramps speed gently by `baseSpeed * 0.9^(level-1)` down to a 60 ms floor (line 1563) βÄî versus classic mode's `/2` halving.
- **Fallback safety**: Any failure to generate a shape or to seat snakes falls back to `boardMask = null` (a full rectangle) so the game always launches (1500βÄ™1503, 1550βÄ™1555, 768βÄ™776, 1023βÄ™1027).

### 3. Board shape representation and bot access

- **Representation**: `boardMask` is a flat `Uint8Array` of length `gridSize*gridSize`, `1 = playable`, `0 = wall/off-board`; `null` means classic full rectangle (fields at lines 78, comment 73βÄ™76). Indexed as `mask[y * gridSize + x]`. `boardArea` (line 79) holds the playable-cell count, used as the capacity metric.
- **Single source of truth**: `onBoard(x,y)` (1320βÄ™1324) checks grid bounds then the mask; `inBounds()` (1326) delegates to it. Collision (`wouldCollide` 1375, `checkCollision` 1451), food placement (`onBoard` in `spawnFood`), and the bot BFS (`analyzePosition`, line 1440) all call `inBounds`, so they automatically respect the Iron Snake shape.
- **Bot access βÄî important nuance**: Bots receive a `BotState` (bot-types.ts lines 3βÄ™9) containing only `snake`, `food`, `gridSize`, `direction`, and optional `opponentSnake` βÄî **the mask itself is NOT exposed to bots**. Bots interact with the board only indirectly through `BotHelpers` (bot-types.ts 17βÄ™25), built at main.ts 1229βÄ™1234 (P1) and 1259βÄ™1275 (P2):
  - `simulateMove` βÜ£ `this.simulateMove` βÜ£ `wouldCollide` βÜ£ `inBounds` βÜ£ mask, so a move onto a wall returns `null`.
  - `analyzePosition` βÜ£ `this.analyzePosition`, whose BFS flood-fill honors `inBounds` (line 1440), so `reachableArea`/`canReachTail`/`pathToFood` correctly reflect the carved shape.
  - `chooseBotDirection` re-validates the chosen direction with `simulateMove` (1240) / a collision recheck (1283βÄ™1286), so bots cannot legally be steered into a wall.
  - **However, bot heuristics that reason about walls directly from `state.gridSize` do NOT see interior walls.** Several bots compute "distance to wall/edge" as `gridSize - 1 - x` etc., assuming a full rectangle: `edge-runner-bot.ts` (lines 17βÄ™44), `spiral-bot.ts` (29βÄ™53), `explorer-bot.ts` (18βÄ™19), `zigzag-bot.ts` (48βÄ™55), `sweeper-bot.ts` (46), `ambusher-bot.ts` (18,32). On an irregular mask these bots misjudge where walls are βÄî they won't crash illegally (helpers still validate), but their positional scoring treats only the bounding-box border as walls, so they can play suboptimally or trap themselves against interior walls they cannot "see."

### 4. Food/points spawning in Iron Snake Mode

`spawnFood()` (1576βÄ™1611):
- When `boardMask !== null` (always in Iron Snake) βÄî or the board is >50% full βÄî it takes the **enumerate-empty-cells** branch (1582βÄ™1598): it iterates all cells, keeps only `onBoard(x,y)` cells not occupied by either snake, and picks uniformly at random. Rejection sampling (1600βÄ™1610) is used only in classic mode. This guarantees **food never spawns on an off-board/wall cell**.
- **Reachability**: The generator guarantees the playable region is a single 4-connected component (`keepLargestComponent`) with no corridor narrower than 3 cells (`passesMinWidth`). So a spawned food is always in the same connected region as the snake and is never in a statically 1βÄ™2-wide "tight spot." Food therefore cannot spawn in a structurally unreachable pocket.
- **Caveats**: (a) The spawn logic does **not** check reachability relative to the *current snake body* βÄî as in classic Snake, the snake can dynamically wall off a region and food could land in a pocket the snake has temporarily sealed. (b) If no empty on-board cell exists (board full), it leaves food where it is (comment line 1596). (c) There is no bias away from walls, so food can spawn directly adjacent to interior walls βÄî which, combined with the bot-heuristic blind spot in section 3, can lure wall-unaware bots into trouble.

There is one point/goal per level: `checkLevelAdvance` (1539) compares combined score to `levelGoal`; food value itself is scored elsewhere (standard per-food scoring), and the cumulative goal formula is `ironSnakeGoalForLevel` (line 27).</result>

Both agents are done. The root cause is clear: bots are **stateless per-tick greedy scorers** with no memory, no randomness, and a single fixed food target βÄî so identical board configurations always produce identical (losing) choices, causing approach/retreat cycles. Let me read the critical files to design a precise fix.

Now I'll add the `resetLoopMemory` calls. First, at the top of `repositionSnakes` (covers iron placement, level advance, grid-resize):

Build passes clean. Now let me verify the loop-guard behavior with a focused simulation driving the real module. `loop-guard.ts` has no runtime deps, so I can exercise it directly.

No TS runner. Let me compile `loop-guard.ts` standalone (it only imports types, which erase) and run a simulation with Node.

My synthetic scenario didn't actually loop (the direction-reversal exclusion prevented tight bouncing). Emergent loops are fragile to reproduce synthetically βÄî let me instead directly unit-test the guard's three mechanisms, which is what actually matters.

All 10 mechanism tests pass. Now let me build a genuine end-to-end oscillation βÄî a ring corridor around walled-off food with one exit βÄî to confirm the guard breaks a *real* cycle in context.

The greedy bot gets stuck (breaks) rather than sustaining a loop βÄî length-1 maze bots don't oscillate like real bodied snakes. Let me demonstrate with a bot that deterministically circles a ring (the exact bug class: repeating cyclic choices) with one exit, and confirm the guard escapes.

`loopFlags=0` βÄî a real finding. My detector (3 revisits within a 30-window) can't catch a 20-cell circuit: on a period-20 loop, no cell recurs 3Ο½ inside 30 moves. The user's "navigate away and return" cycle can easily be that long. I need a confinement-ratio detector too. Let me strengthen `isLooping`.

Now recompile and re-run all three verifications.

Now the confinement detector catches the 20-cell circuit: the guard breaks the ring bot out from 20 cells to 125 explored. Let me run the full production build to confirm everything still typechecks.

All verifications green. Let me mark the task complete.

Done. Here's what I built and verified.

## The bug
Every bot is a **stateless, per-tick greedy scorer** with no memory, no randomness, and no tie-breaking (`src/bots/*.ts`). There's a single fixed food target. So for an identical board configuration, the scoring always yields the identical move βÄî any positional cycle is stable and never breaks. Iron Snake's irregular masks create many tight pockets, which is why it surfaces there: the bot approaches food, correctly refuses to enter a trap, retreats, then re-picks the exact same losing approach forever.

## The fix
A centralized **loop guard** (`src/bots/loop-guard.ts`) βÄî the memory + variation the bot layer lacks βÄî wired into both game harnesses. It's transparent during normal play and only engages when a loop is actually detected, so all 11 bot personalities stay intact.

- **Detection** (`isLooping`): two signals βÄî a tight oscillation (a cell revisited 3Ο½ in the window) *or* confinement (over a 40-move window, too few distinct cells relative to moves). The confinement check was added after a test caught that a pure revisit-counter can't detect a ~20-cell "wander away and return" circuit.
- **Escape** (`chooseEscapeDirection`): among legal, non-trapping moves (`canReachTail`), steer toward the least-recently-visited cell, ties broken randomly βÄî driving the snake into unexplored space. Commits for 8 ticks so it actually leaves rather than snapping back.
- **Reset** on every progress event (food eaten, level advance, reposition, new game) so stale history never causes spurious escapes.

**Files:** new `src/bots/loop-guard.ts`; wiring + memory fields + reset calls in `src/main.ts` (single-player, both BvB players); same for the title-screen demo in `src/menu-demo.ts`.

## Verification
- `npm run build` (tsc + vite) passes clean.
- 10 unit tests against the real module: detection threshold, no false-positive on forward progress, reset, escape steering toward least-visited, trap-avoidance, transparency in normal play, engage-and-commit on loop βÄî all pass.
- End-to-end sim of a bot stuck circling a 20-cell ring: without the guard it stays on 20 cells forever; **with the guard it detects the loop and explores 125 cells.**

One thing worth flagging for a real playtest: the guard reliably *breaks* loops, but on a genuinely unreachable food (snake temporarily walled off a pocket) the bot will wander productively rather than solve the impossible βÄî which is the correct behavior. To see it live: `npm run dev`, enable Iron Snake Mode with the Survival or Hunter bot, and watch it wander off instead of oscillating in tight pockets.

I don't want the bot to get into a state where it can't win.. but also will never lose forcing the watcher to stop the game.  if a bot wanders for too long it needs to lose. what is a good way to facilite this?

Good question βÄî this is the right complement to the loop guard. The guard stops the *frozen* oscillation, but now a bot could wander productively forever without ever dying. Let me look at the game-over paths, the tick loop, and the HUD before recommending a mechanism.

Let me read the endGame paths and the HUD/updateUI to understand how a loss is triggered and displayed.

Now I understand the game-over paths and HUD. Two-player already routes wins/losses/draws through `endGameTwoPlayer(p1Dead, p2Dead)`, so a starving snake just sets its "dead" flag. Let me quickly check how the snake is drawn (to gauge a visual warning) before I recommend.

Here's my read, then two decisions for you.

## Recommended mechanism: a starvation timer

Track **moves-since-last-food** per snake. Reset it to 0 whenever that snake eats (and on new game / level advance / reposition βÄî the same progress points the loop guard already hooks). When the counter exceeds a limit, that snake dies through the existing game-over paths.

Key properties that make this safe and fair:
- **The limit scales with board area** βÄî `max(floor, K Ο½ boardArea)`. Because it resets on every food, a competent bot on a reachable board never approaches it; it only fires when food is genuinely walled off or the bot is hopelessly stuck. It's a backstop, not constant pressure.
- **Reuses existing loss logic** βÄî in Bot-vs-Bot, a starved snake just sets its `p1Dead`/`p2Dead` flag, so `endGameTwoPlayer` already declares the opponent the winner (or a draw if both starve at once).
- **Pairs with the loop guard** βÄî the guard keeps the snake exploring so it *finds* reachable food; starvation guarantees a clean, automatic loss when the food truly can't be reached. Together: it can't freeze, and it can't wander forever.

Two design forks genuinely change what I build:

Both decisions noted: **all snakes** can starve, and I'll add a **visual hunger tint + "Starved!" game-over reason**. Let me check that `boardArea` stays correct across grid expansion (it feeds the limit), then implement.

`expandGrid()` doubles `gridSize` but never refreshes `boardArea` βÄî a latent staleness that would make my area-based limit wrong after a classic expansion. I'll fix that too. Let me view the constants block and field area for placement.

Now I have everything. Let me set up tasks and implement. First, the constants and the color-lerp helper.

Now the fields, next to the stats tracking:

Now two-player: increment/reset counters and route starvation deaths through `endGameTwoPlayer`.

Now add the starvation death check at the end of the two-player tick. Let me view the tail end of `updateTwoPlayer`:

Now the resets and the `boardArea` fix. New-game reset:

Tick wiring done. Now the hunger tint in `draw()` and the game-over reasons.

Now the game-over reasons. Single-player title:

Now the two-player winner text βÄî augment to indicate starvation. Let me view that block:

Now build and verify.

Build passes. Now let me verify the starvation timing, hunger ramp, and color-lerp formulas behave correctly by replicating the exact logic from `main.ts`.

All 14 pass. Let me do a final review pass βÄî confirm every `endGame`/`endGameTwoPlayer` call site interacts correctly with `deathCause` (no stale 'starved' leaking into a collision death).

All call sites are consistent: `deathCause` is reset to `'collision'` at new-game (line 1104), and starvation is the only thing that sets `'starved'` βÄî and it ends the game immediately, so a collision death can never carry a stale `'starved'`.

Let me mark verification complete.

## Done βÄî starvation timer added

A snake that goes too long without eating now dies, so a stuck or endlessly-wandering bot loses cleanly instead of forcing you to stop the game.

**How it works**
- **Per-snake `movesSinceFood` counter**, reset to 0 on eating (and on new game / level advance / reposition βÄî the same progress points the loop guard already uses).
- **Limit scales with playable area:** `max(100, 2 Ο½ boardArea)`. Because it resets on every food, a competent snake on a reachable board never approaches it βÄî it only fires when food is genuinely walled off or the bot is hopelessly stuck. I also fixed a latent bug where `expandGrid()` doubled `gridSize` without refreshing `boardArea`, which would have made the limit wrong after a classic-mode expansion.
- **Reuses the existing loss logic:** single-player calls `endGame()`; Bot-vs-Bot feeds the starved snake as "dead" into `endGameTwoPlayer`, so the opponent wins (or it's a draw if both starve on the same tick).
- **Applies to all snakes** (per your choice), including human players in P-vs-P.

**Legibility (per your choice)**
- The snake **tints from its normal colour toward amber (head) / red (body)** as it crosses 60% of the limit, ramping to full warning colour at death βÄî so it's visibly "starving," not an abrupt disappearance.
- The game-over screen shows **"Starved!"** (single) or e.g. **"Player 2 Wins! (P1 starved)"** / **"Draw βÄî both starved!"** (2P).

**How it pairs with the loop guard:** the guard keeps the snake exploring so it *finds* reachable food; starvation guarantees a clean, automatic loss when the food truly can't be reached. Together the bot can't freeze *and* can't wander forever.

**Verification**
- `npm run build` (tsc typecheck + vite bundle) passes clean.
- 14 logic assertions pass against the exact formulas: limit scaling + floor, starves at exactly the limit, eating-in-time never starves, hunger ramp (0 before 60%, exactly 1 at the limit, clamped past it), color-lerp endpoints, and no divide-by-zero at the floor limit.

**Tunables** (all constants near the top of `src/main.ts`): `STARVATION_AREA_FACTOR` (2), `STARVATION_FLOOR` (100), `STARVATION_WARN_FRACTION` (0.6), and the two warning palettes. The title-screen demo intentionally doesn't starve βÄî it's decorative and already auto-restarts.

One thing worth a real playtest to tune by feel: on the default 10Ο½10 board the limit is 200 moves, which at 200 ms/tick is ~40 s of no progress before death. If that feels too long to watch, drop `STARVATION_AREA_FACTOR` to 1.5 or the floor lower.

A pulsing alert now appears the instant a snake enters the hunger zone βÄî the same 60%-of-limit threshold where the color tint begins, so the notification and the tint start together.

**What I added**
- **Overlay banner** (`index.html`): a `βö† Starving βÄî reach the food!` pill wrapped over the canvas in a `.canvas-wrap`, so it appears *without shifting the game layout*.
- **Pulsing style** (`style.css`): amber-bordered red pill with a `starve-pulse` animation (fades/glows on an 0.8s loop) to grab attention; `pointer-events: none` so it never interferes with play.

looks good.  can you remind me how this works again.. the more food the snake has the longer it can go?  aka, longer snakes can last longer?

Happy to βÄî and it's worth clearing up, because it's a natural assumption but **not** how it actually works. Length doesn't factor in at all.

## What the limit actually depends on

```
starvationLimit = max(100, 2 Ο½ boardArea)
```

- `STARVATION_FLOOR = 100` βÄî never starve in fewer than 100 moves
- `STARVATION_AREA_FACTOR = 2` βÄî 2 moves of budget per playable cell
- **`boardArea`** = number of playable cells (the whole grid in classic mode; the carved-out area in Iron Snake)

So the budget is set by **how big the board is**, not how long the snake is.

## Why it *feels* like longer snakes last longer

The counter is `movesSinceFood`, and **it resets to 0 every time the snake eats**. So each meal buys a fresh, full budget again.

- A snake that keeps eating in time can live **forever** βÄî not because its budget grows, but because the clock keeps getting reset.
- A snake gets exactly the same budget on its 1st food as on its 50th. Eating doesn't *extend* a hunger window; it *restarts* it.

So the accurate framing is: **"as long as you eat again within ~N moves, you're fine"** βÄî where N is the fixed per-board budget. It's not "more food = a longer leash."

## Concrete numbers

| Board | boardArea | Budget between meals | Warning banner appears at (60%) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Default 10Ο½10 | 100 | 200 moves | 120 moves |
| Iron Snake 12Ο½12 (carved) | ~60βÄ™108 | ~120βÄ™216 moves | ~72βÄ™130 moves |

The one thing that *does* grow the budget mid-game is the **board getting bigger** βÄî classic-mode grid expansion and Iron Snake level regeneration both update `boardArea`, so the limit rises with the new (larger) board. That's the fairness reason it's area-based: a bigger board means food can legitimately be farther away.

If you'd actually *prefer* the behavior you described βÄî longer snakes getting more breathing room βÄî that's a one-line change: make the limit `max(100, 2 Ο½ boardArea) + snakeLength Ο½ K`, or scale by current length. Want me to add that?
Sounds good βÄî leaving it as is. The area-based limit with reset-on-eat stays. Let me know if you want to revisit anything.
